skill.name: torch-geometric
skill.description: Build and adapt PyTorch Geometric workflows for graph neural networks, including node classification, graph classification, link prediction, datasets, transforms, and visualization.
skill.summary: Create PyTorch Geometric graph learning models, dataset workflows, benchmarks, and visualizations.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer produced many high and critical alerts, but review found they are false positives from markdown code fences, PyTorch model.eval() calls, and dataset reference text. No prompt injection, credential access, shell execution, or data exfiltration intent was found. The remaining risk is legitimate utility behavior: runnable scripts can download public PyG datasets and write user-selected model or image files.

medium_findings.title: Runnable Utilities Can Download Public Datasets
medium_findings.description: The benchmark and visualization scripts load PyTorch Geometric datasets using dataset names supplied through command-line arguments. This is expected for a PyG skill, but execution may contact dataset hosts and store dataset files locally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code directly constructs PyG dataset objects under /tmp from command-line dataset names. This is legitimate ML workflow behavior, but it has network and filesystem side effects when scripts are executed.

medium_findings.title: User-Selected Output Paths Write Files
medium_findings.description: The template generator and visualization utilities write files to paths provided by the caller. This is useful behavior, but users should review generated paths before running commands suggested by an assistant.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and depend on user-supplied output values. No malicious destination or hidden write was found, so the concern is operational rather than hostile.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick alerts are caused by markdown inline code and fenced Python examples, not executable shell command invocation. No evidence of subprocess, os.system, or shell backtick execution was found in the cited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are markdown prose or code examples. A repository search found no shell command execution APIs in the cited skill files.

low_findings.title: Static eval Alerts Are PyTorch Evaluation Mode False Positives
low_findings.description: The dynamic code execution findings point to model.eval(), which switches PyTorch modules into evaluation mode. It is not Python eval() and does not execute user-provided code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each cited location uses a PyTorch model method named eval. There is no Python eval call at these locations.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Domain Term False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts appear to match graph-learning terms, dataset names, model names, and normal ML code tokens. No hashing, encryption, certificate handling, or cryptographic algorithm use was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled alert locations are ML documentation or examples, not cryptographic code. No evidence found for weak crypto usage in executable scripts.

dangerous_patterns.title: File Write From Command-Line Output Argument
dangerous_patterns.description: The template generator writes generated Python code to a caller-provided output path. This is transparent utility behavior, but it should be treated as a file-writing capability when assistants invoke the script.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write call and output path are explicit. The pattern is not malicious, but it is a real filesystem side effect.

content.user_title: Build Graph Neural Networks with PyG
content.value_statement: Graph neural network projects require many PyG-specific choices across data objects, layers, datasets, and training loops. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused PyTorch Geometric guidance, templates, benchmarks, and visualization utilities.
actual_capabilities: Explains PyTorch Geometric data objects, edge indexes, batching, masks, and custom dataset patterns.
Guides model selection for GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, GIN, heterogeneous graphs, link prediction, and graph classification.
Provides bundled references for PyG datasets, transforms, layer capabilities, and common API patterns.
Includes a benchmark script for comparing GNN models on standard Planetoid and TUDataset datasets.
Includes a template generator for node classification and graph classification model files.
Includes graph visualization utilities for structure, degree distributions, and summary statistics.
limitations: It does not install PyTorch, PyG, CUDA, or optional sparse operation packages automatically.
Benchmark results depend on local hardware, package versions, random seeds, and dataset availability.
Generated templates are starting points and still need review for production training workflows.
Some bundled dataset examples may download public data when executed.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Citation Network Classifier
use_cases.description: Create a Cora, CiteSeer, or PubMed node classification model with PyG data loading, masks, and training loops.

use_cases.title: Compare GNN Architectures for Research
use_cases.description: Benchmark GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE models with consistent training settings and clear result summaries.

use_cases.title: Inspect Graph Dataset Structure
use_cases.description: Visualize graph topology, degree distributions, and dataset statistics before model development.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic PyG Data Object
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small custom graph.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the torch-geometric skill to show me how to create a PyG Data object for a small graph with node features, labels, and edge_index.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Node Classifier
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the torch-geometric skill to create a GCN node classification workflow for Cora, including data loading, model definition, training, and evaluation.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Layers for My Graph Task
prompt_templates.scenario: Model selection across graph structure and feature constraints.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the torch-geometric skill to compare GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, and GIN for my graph dataset. Explain which layer fits node classification, graph classification, and edge attributes.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Advanced PyG Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: End-to-end graph ML workflow with transforms and benchmarking.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the torch-geometric skill to design an end-to-end PyG pipeline with dataset transforms, mini-batching, model benchmarking, visualization, and checks for data leakage.

output_examples.input: Create a GCN workflow for node classification on Cora.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step PyG plan with dataset loading, model layers, optimizer settings, train and test masks, evaluation metrics, and notes about avoiding leakage.

output_examples.input: Compare GAT and GraphSAGE for my social graph.
output: A practical comparison of attention-based and sampling-friendly approaches.
Recommendations based on graph size, feature quality, neighbor counts, and compute budget.
Suggested benchmark settings for a fair local test.

output_examples.input: Help me inspect a graph dataset before training.
output_examples.output: A review checklist covering node and edge counts, feature dimensions, label distribution, homophily assumptions, split strategy, and visualization options.

best_practices: Verify train, validation, and test masks before reporting accuracy or benchmark results.
Keep dataset roots and generated output paths explicit when running bundled scripts.
Start with a small baseline model before adding attention, heterogeneity, or custom message passing.
anti_patterns: Do not compare GNN models with different splits, epochs, or preprocessing unless the difference is intentional.
Do not run generated templates blindly on private data without reviewing paths, transforms, and saved outputs.
Do not assume every PyG dataset has the same masks, labels, feature shapes, or graph-level targets.

faq.question: Does this skill train models by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and utility scripts. You run training in your own Python environment.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it include runnable scripts?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes scripts for benchmarking models, generating templates, and visualizing graphs.

faq.question: Will the scripts access the network?
faq.answer: They may download public PyTorch Geometric datasets when dataset constructors are executed.

faq.question: Can I use it for molecular graphs?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation covers molecular property prediction datasets and graph learning patterns.

faq.question: Is it production ready?
faq.answer: It is best treated as a development aid. Review generated code, dependency versions, and dataset handling before production use.
